title: Psalms 39
subtitle: Show Me How Fleeting My Life Is

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — “I Am a Stranger Before You”

heb.paragraph: "I will put a muzzle (מַחְסוֹם) on my mouth" (v.1). The noun מַחְסוֹם appears only here in the Hebrew Bible — a hapax legomenon. It derives from חָסַם (ḥāsam), 'to stop up, muzzle.' The psalmist pictures himself physically restraining his speech as one would restrain an animal. The image is violent self-control: not gentle silence but forcible suppression of words that might give the wicked ammunition.

"Everyone is but a breath (הֶבֶל)" (v.5, 11). This is the signature word of Ecclesiastes — 'vanity of vanities' — but here in a psalm of lament. הֶבֶל means breath, vapor, something that appears and vanishes. The psalmist uses it three times (vv.5, 6, 11) to hammer home human transience. We are not merely mortal; we are mist. The word carries no moral judgment — it simply names the unbearable lightness of being.

"You have made my days a mere handbreadth (טְפָחוֹת)" (v.5). A טֶפַח (ṭepaḥ) is the width of four fingers — roughly three inches. It's the smallest unit of Hebrew linear measurement. The psalmist doesn't say his life is short; he says it's the smallest measurable length. God has measured out his days with a ruler, and the total is barely worth counting.

"Everyone goes around like a mere phantom (צֶלֶם)" (v.6). The word צֶלֶם famously means 'image' in Genesis 1:26-27 — humanity made in God's image. Here it means shadow, phantom, mere appearance. The irony is devastating: the image-bearers have become images themselves, insubstantial projections. They bustle about accumulating wealth that will pass to unknown heirs. Form without substance.

refs.note: Moses' prayer shares the same meditation on human transience: 'You turn people back to dust … our days may come to seventy years.' Both psalms measure human life against God's eternity and find it vanishingly brief.
James echoes v. 5: 'What is your life? You are a mist that appears for a little while and then vanishes.' The psalm's metaphor of breath (hebel) becomes James' image of vapour.
The repeated term hebel (breath, vapour) connects this psalm to Ecclesiastes' governing metaphor. Both texts use the same word to express the evanescence of human existence.

notes.note: MacArthur: David's attempt to remain silent before the wicked illustrates the tension between godly restraint (Prov 10:19) and the believer's need to pour out his heart to God. Restraint toward men should not become silence toward God.
MacArthur: the prayer for awareness of life's brevity (cf. Ps 90:12, James 4:14) is a recurring biblical theme. Understanding our mortality produces humility, urgency, and proper dependence on God.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'I will put a muzzle on my mouth' (maḥsôm) — the image is violent. The psalmist restrains his speech not gently but forcefully, as one muzzles an animal. The urge to speak is almost uncontrollable. This is not serene silence but desperate self-restraint in the presence of the wicked.

Alter: 'each man's life is but a breath' (hebel) — the same word Ecclesiastes uses sixty times. Here as there, it means vapour, exhalation — something visible for a moment, then nothing. Human existence at its most solid is still mist. The repetition of hebel three times (vv.5, 6, 11) drives home the psalm's central insight.

Calvin: the resolve to be silent fails — the fire burns and the words break out. Calvin: this is honest self-reporting. The psalmist tried to suppress his complaint and could not. Repression does not work; only honest prayer does. The 'fire' that burns within is grief seeking expression, not sinful anger.

Calvin: 'show me, Lord, my life's end and the number of my days' — Calvin reads this not as morbid curiosity but as a prayer for perspective. Knowledge of brevity is the beginning of wisdom. The psalmist wants to feel his mortality so that he might live with appropriate humility and urgency.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'while in the presence of the wicked' — the psalmist's silence is strategic, not universal. He restrains complaint specifically to avoid giving the wicked ammunition. His suffering should not become their entertainment or occasion for mocking God. The wisdom is knowing when speech helps and when it harms.

NET: hebel ('breath, vapour') carries connotations of both brevity and insubstantiality. Life is not merely short but lightweight — lacking the permanence and significance humans desperately wish it had. The word appears in all three major wisdom books: Psalms, Proverbs, and Ecclesiastes.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the failed silence is psychologically acute. Bottled-up grief does not disappear; it intensifies. The fire metaphor is exact: suppressed emotion heats up until it erupts. Better to speak to God than to explode before the wicked. The psalmist's honesty about his struggle models authentic prayer.

Kidner: 'a mere phantom' (ṣelem) — humanity as shadow, image without substance. The word echoes Genesis 1:26-27 (made in God's ṣelem) with bitter irony: the image of God is itself an image, a projection, a shadow. We bear divine dignity yet dissolve like morning mist.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the prayer for awareness of mortality is a wisdom-psalm motif (cf. Ps 90:12). Knowing one's finitude is not despair but the foundation of dependence on God. Mortality drives the psalmist toward the Eternal. The request is not for the date of death but for the reality of death to shape daily living.

VanGemeren: the hebel language connects Psalm 39 to Ecclesiastes' theology. But where Ecclesiastes explores hebel philosophically, the psalmist experiences it existentially — in illness, isolation, and divine discipline. Theory becomes biography under the weight of suffering.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the muzzle (maḥsôm) is a hapax legomenon — used only here in the Hebrew Bible. The rare word emphasises the extreme measure the psalmist takes. This is not ordinary discretion but desperate self-restraint. The uniqueness of the word matches the extremity of the situation.

Goldingay: 'busy about nothing' — the hebel of human activity means that accumulation and ambition are equally vaporous. The psalmist does not condemn work but questions its ultimate significance when death cancels all accounts. Wealth passes to unknown heirs; legacy evaporates.

hist.context: Psalm 39 reflects on human mortality with an existential intensity unusual even for the Psalter. The psalmist initially resolves to remain silent to avoid sinning with his tongue (vv.1-3), but the internal pressure of grief becomes unbearable and erupts in speech. The cultural context includes the ancient Near Eastern tradition of lament before the deity, where expressing suffering was considered a legitimate religious act rather than a sign of weak faith. The 'breath' and 'shadow' imagery (vv.5-6, 11) echoes Ecclesiastes' hebel vocabulary, suggesting shared participation in Israel's wisdom tradition of confronting life's brevity.

sections.header: Verses 7–13 — “Look Away from Me Before I Depart”

heb.paragraph: "My hope (תּוֹחַלְתִּי) is in you" (v.7). After cataloging human vanity, the psalmist pivots sharply: 'But now, LORD, what do I wait for?' The noun תּוֹחֶלֶת (tôḥelet) means hope, expectation, waiting. It implies patient endurance, not passive resignation. If life is vapor, what remains? Only YHWH. The question is rhetorical — there is nothing else worth hoping in.

"I dwell with you as a foreigner (גֵּר)" (v.12). A גֵּר was a resident alien in Israel — protected by law but never fully belonging. The psalmist claims this status before God: 'I am a stranger in your house.' This is not alienation but humility. The patriarchs were גֵּרִים (Gen 23:4); Israel was גֵּר in Egypt (Exod 22:21). To be a stranger is to depend entirely on the host's hospitality.

"A stranger (גֵּר), a sojourner (תּוֹשָׁב), as all my ancestors were" (v.12). The תּוֹשָׁב is even more temporary than the גֵּר — a passing guest rather than a resident alien. The double term emphasizes transience: not even a permanent immigrant, just passing through. Abraham used both words when negotiating Sarah's burial plot (Gen 23:4). The psalmist inherits his ancestors' landlessness before God.

"Look away (הָשַׁע) from me, that I may enjoy life again" (v.13). The imperative הָשַׁע (from שָׁעָה, šāʿâ) means 'turn your gaze away.' This is the opposite of the usual plea — typically the psalmist begs God not to hide his face (Ps 27:9). Here, God's attention has become unbearable scrutiny. The psalmist wants a moment's respite before death. Even divine presence can feel like judgment when we are conscious of our vapor-like existence.

refs.note: The confession 'I dwell with you as a foreigner, a stranger, as all my ancestors were' (v. 12) echoes Psalm 119:19: 'I am a stranger on earth.' The resident-alien metaphor describes the life of faith itself.
David's prayer at the temple offering uses the same language: 'We are foreigners and strangers in your sight, as were all our ancestors.' The sentiment passes from private psalm to public liturgy.
Peter addresses believers as 'foreigners and exiles' — the psalm's self-description becomes a defining category for the entire Christian community's relationship to the world.

notes.note: MacArthur: after stripping away every earthly confidence, David arrives at the only reliable foundation: 'My hope is in you.' This is the proper conclusion of meditating on life's brevity — not despair but redirected trust.

MacArthur: identifying as a 'stranger and sojourner' reflects the biblical truth that believers are citizens of heaven living temporarily on earth (Phil 3:20, Heb 11:13). This perspective transforms how we handle loss and suffering.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'And now, Lord, what do I look for?' — the pivot of the psalm. Having declared life vapour and activity futile, the psalmist has only one option left: hope in God. Despair about the world becomes dependence on the Eternal. The question is rhetorical; the answer is already forming.

Alter: 'a stranger…a sojourner' (gēr…tôšāv) — the psalmist claims the most vulnerable social categories as self-descriptions. Before God, we have no permanent address, no citizen's rights. We are guests in God's house, dependent on divine hospitality for our very existence.

Calvin: when everything else is declared hebel, only God remains. Calvin: the stripping away of false hopes is painful but necessary. What survives the fire of disillusionment is the only thing worth hoping in. The psalm is a controlled demolition of idols leading to one remaining foundation.

Calvin: 'Look away from me, that I may enjoy life again' — the most startling request in the Psalter. The psalmist wants God to stop looking at him. Calvin: this is not rejection of God but exhaustion under God's scrutiny. The sufferer needs breathing room, a moment's respite from divine attention.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'you rebuke and discipline anyone for their sin; you consume their wealth like a moth' — the moth image conveys silent, gradual destruction. God's discipline is not always dramatic; sometimes it works like decay, imperceptibly reducing what seemed secure. The psalmist experiences this slow erosion personally.

NET: 'a stranger…a sojourner, as all my ancestors were' — the language echoes 1 Chronicles 29:15 and Leviticus 25:23. Even in the promised land, Israel held the land as God's guests, not as owners. The theological category shapes the existential experience of vulnerability.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the turning point. Everything has been declared futile — life, activity, wealth. The only thing left standing is God. Kidner: the psalm strips away every false support until faith stands alone. What looks like despair is actually purification. Hope in God is the residue after all else burns away.

Kidner: 'before I depart and am no more' — the psalm ends in shadow. No praise, no resolution, no lifting of the burden. Like Psalm 88, it refuses a tidy ending. Kidner: Scripture validates prayers that end in the dark. Not every night ends in morning within the psalm itself.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the stranger-sojourner motif is central to Israel's self-understanding (cf. 1 Pet 2:11). The people of God are always pilgrims — in Egypt, in the wilderness, and even in the land. Home is eschatological, not geographical. The psalmist joins a long line of wanderers trusting a promise.

VanGemeren: the unresolved ending, like Psalm 88, resists easy comfort. The canon preserves these dark prayers because they are true to human experience. Not every night ends in morning within the psalm itself. The resolution comes from outside — from the God who hears even despairing cries.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the question 'what do I look for?' is both existential and practical. Having declared everything hebel, the psalmist must decide where to invest what remains of his hope. The answer — 'my hope is in you' — is the psalm's quiet revolution. From despair about everything comes trust in One.

Goldingay: 'Look away from me' — Job 7:19 makes the same request. The sufferer experiences God's attention not as comfort but as pressure. The desire for God's absence is paradoxically an acknowledgment of God's overwhelming reality. Only a God who is terrifyingly present could be asked to look away.

hist.context: The phrase 'I am a stranger with you, a sojourner like all my ancestors' (v.12) draws on the patriarchal tradition of landlessness (Genesis 23:4; 47:9; Leviticus 25:23). In the ancient Near East, resident aliens (gērîm) had legal protections but no permanent claim — the psalmist applies this vulnerable status to the human condition before God. The startling request 'look away from me, that I may enjoy life before I depart and am no more' (v.13) is one of the most theologically daring prayers in the Psalter, asking God to reduce his scrutiny rather than increase his attention.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: One wrestling with the brevity and seeming futility of life (vv.1-6,12-13)
ppl.text: David initially resolves silence — 'I will put a muzzle on my mouth while in the presence of the wicked' (v.1) — but his anguish bursts out: 'Show me, LORD, my life's end and the number of my days; let me know how fleeting my life is' (v.4). Life is measured in 'handbreadths' (tefachim, v.5) — the smallest unit of Hebrew measurement. This is Ecclesiastes-level existential honesty within the Psalter, and it ends with a startling request: 'Look away from me, that I may enjoy life again before I depart and am no more' (v.13).

ppl.name: God (Yahweh)
ppl.role: The discipliner whose silence David both requests and dreads (vv.9-11)
ppl.text: David attributes his suffering to God's direct action: 'You have done this' (v.9). God's 'rebuke and discipline' consume human beauty 'like a moth' (v.11) — an image of slow, inexorable destruction. Yet David calls himself 'a foreigner, a stranger' before God (v.12), echoing Abraham's self-description (Gen 23:4) and establishing a theology of pilgrim identity that Hebrews 11:13 later develops.

ppl.name: Jeduthun
ppl.role: Temple music director named in the superscription
ppl.text: The psalm is assigned 'to the director of music, to Jeduthun.' Jeduthun was one of three Levitical music leaders appointed by David (1 Chr 25:1-6), alongside Asaph and Heman. His name appears in Psalms 39, 62, and 77 superscriptions. The connection suggests this deeply personal lament was incorporated into Israel's public worship repertoire — private anguish becoming communal liturgy.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI said, “I will watch my ways and keep my tongue from sin; I will put a muzzle on my mouth while in the presence of the wicked.”ESVI said, \u201cI will guard my ways, that I may not sin with my tongue; I will guard my mouth with a muzzle, so long as the wicked are in my presence.\u201dNIVI was silent; I would not open my mouth, for you are the one who has done this.ESVI am mute; I do not open my mouth, for it is you who have done it.

rows.label: The Stranger — 39:7–13
rows.text: "I am a foreigner with you." "Look away from me before I depart." Temporary residence.

hebtext.note: "Each person’s life is but a breath (hebel)" (v.5). Ecclesiastes’ signature word appears in the Psalter. David tried to keep silent but his anguish grew until he spoke (vv.1–3). His request: "Show me, LORD, my life’s end and the number of my days; let me know how fleeting my life is" (v.4). The response: a handbreadth. A mere breath. Hebel.

"I dwell with you as a foreigner (gēr), a stranger, as all my ancestors were" (v.12). Life on earth is temporary residence. David is a guest, not an owner. The psalm closes with a remarkable request: "Look away from me, that I may enjoy life again, before I depart and am no more" (v.13). David wants God to relent before death comes.

themes.note: Sovereignty: life’s brevity is God’s design. The hebel theology bridges Ecclesiastes and the Psalter. Faith: living as a stranger before God.

prayer_prompt: As you consider 'Show me, LORD, my life's end and the number of my days,' consider asking God to give you a sober awareness of how brief your life is — not to depress you but to sharpen your priorities.
